title: Transporting sheep on a small holding
subtitle: Loading, movement records, and minimising stress on the journey.
excerpt: Moving sheep off the holding, whether to the abattoir, a show, a new field, or a neighbour's land, triggers the 6-day movement standstill for most trips and requires a completed eAML2 or equivalent movement document. Loading calmly takes a little preparation but goes faster than a chaotic rush.
sourceNotes: DEFRA livestock movement rules, gov.uk. AHDB Beef & Lamb transport guidance. Animal Transport Regulations 2006 (UK). APHA movement standstill guidance.

glossaryTerms.definition: A mandatory period after sheep, goats, or cattle arrive at a new holding. For sheep and goats this is 6 days; for pigs it is 20 days. During the standstill, no other susceptible animals may leave the holding.
The electronic Animal Movement Licence system used to record the movement of sheep, goats, and pigs in Great Britain. Completed online before the animals move.

content.text: Most sheep movements off a holding in England and Wales require a completed

movement licence before the animals move. The exception is within a single county or parish if the holding is registered as a common. Even for small movements, incomplete paperwork can result in a formal warning.
When sheep arrive on your holding from elsewhere, a

of 6 days applies. No sheep, goats, or cattle may leave your holding for those 6 days. The standstill does not apply when animals are moved off your holding to slaughter, shows (with the correct show exemption), or veterinary treatment.

Work sheep quietly the day before loading. Rushed penning the morning of a move stresses the animals and makes loading harder. Check all ear tags are in place and legible, untagged or illegibly tagged sheep cannot be legally transported.

Starve sheep of solid feed for twelve hours before a long journey (over two hours). This reduces the weight on the trailer, slows gut motility, and reduces the risk of bloat or diarrhoea on the move. Water access right up to loading is fine.

Set the trailer ramp at a comfortable angle and bed it with straw. Sheep loaded onto a bare metal ramp will panic at the unfamiliar surface. Use a race or temporary hurdle funnel to channel them toward the ramp; a single handler behind at a distance works better than multiple people pushing close to the sheep.

If one sheep gets in, the others usually follow quickly. If the first sheep refuses the ramp after two or three attempts, check the ramp angle, the light inside the trailer (sheep will not walk into darkness from a bright yard), and whether the floor grip is adequate. A small amount of feed at the top of the ramp can help a reluctant leader.

Stocking density in the trailer matters. The legal minimum space per adult sheep is 0.20 to 0.30 square metres depending on weight. Overloaded animals cannot balance properly on a moving vehicle and are more likely to be injured. Underloaded animals can fall over in corners; a single sheep in a half-empty trailer needs a solid partition to stop it moving around.

Check all animals on unloading. Sheep that have been lying in the trailer will sometimes fail to stand immediately. Give them a few minutes before helping them up. Any animal that is lame, obviously injured, or unwilling to bear weight after standing needs to be separated and assessed.
Record the movement in your holding register within 36 hours of arrival. Scotland and Wales have their own rules, which differ from England. Check before moving animals across a national border.
